title: 序章：当你打开一个「聊天助手」的引擎盖

qa.q: Claude Code 到底是什么？它和普通聊天 AI 有什么本质区别？
qa.qEn: What exactly is Claude Code, and how is it fundamentally different from a regular chat AI?
qa.a:
Claude Code 是一个运行在终端（命令行窗口）里的 AI 智能体系统，拥有 1,884 个源代码文件。它和普通聊天 AI 的本质区别在于：普通聊天 AI 只是"对话"——你说一句，它回一句，中间什么都不做。Claude Code 则是一个"行动者"——它可以读你的文件、修改代码、运行命令、管理进程，甚至同时派出多个 AI 子实例并行干活。

打个比方：普通聊天 AI 就像电话客服，只能告诉你怎么做；Claude Code 更像是派到你家里的技术工程师，可以直接动手拆机修复。这个"动手能力"要求系统必须解决一系列普通聊天 AI 不需要面对的问题：操作权限怎么控制、文件改错了怎么回滚、多个任务同时进行怎么协调、对话太长了怎么压缩……这些才是 Claude Code 系统复杂度的真正来源。
qa.aEn:
Claude Code is an AI agent system that runs inside your terminal (command-line window), built from 1,884 source files. The fundamental difference from regular chat AI: a normal chat AI only *talks* — you say something, it replies, and nothing happens in between. Claude Code is an *actor* — it reads your files, edits code, runs commands, manages processes, and can even spawn multiple AI sub-instances to work in parallel.

Think of it this way: a regular chat AI is like phone support — it can only tell you what to do. Claude Code is more like a technician dispatched to your home who can pop open the machine and fix it directly. That "hands-on capability" forces the system to solve a long list of problems a chat AI never has to deal with: how to control permissions, how to roll back a bad edit, how to coordinate parallel tasks, how to compress an overlong conversation. That's the real source of Claude Code's complexity.

qa.q: 书中把 Claude Code 比作"操作系统"，这个比喻是什么意思？为什么这样理解有帮助？
qa.qEn: The book compares Claude Code to an "operating system." What does that metaphor mean, and why is it a useful lens?
qa.a:
操作系统（Windows、macOS）做三件核心事：管理计算机硬件资源（CPU、内存）、调度运行中的程序（进程）、执行安全策略（谁能访问什么文件）。Claude Code 做的事几乎一样，只是管理的对象换了——它管理的"资源"是 AI 的 Token 预算和 API 配额，管理的"程序"是各个 AI 智能体实例，执行的"安全策略"是权限规则和沙箱限制。

这个比喻有帮助是因为它让你用已经熟悉的框架来理解一个全新的系统。比如你知道操作系统有"系统调用"（应用程序请求 OS 帮它干事），Claude Code 也有类似机制——AI 不能直接碰你的文件，它必须发出"工具调用"请求，由本地系统代为执行。这个对应关系一下子就让你理解了为什么要有"工具"这个概念。

当然，作者也明确指出这是帮助理解的类比，不是精确的技术定义——Claude Code 实际运行在 Node.js 之上，本质还是一个用户态程序。
qa.aEn:
An operating system (Windows, macOS) does three core things: manage hardware resources (CPU, memory), schedule running programs (processes), and enforce security policy (who can access which files). Claude Code does nearly the same things — only the objects have changed. The "resources" it manages are token budgets and API quotas; the "programs" are AI agent instances; the "security policy" it enforces is permission rules and sandbox constraints.

The metaphor helps because it lets you understand a brand-new system through a framework you already know. For example, you know an OS has "system calls" (applications asking the OS to do something on their behalf). Claude Code has an equivalent mechanism — the AI can't touch your files directly. It has to issue a "tool call" request, which the local system executes on its behalf. That one correspondence instantly explains why the concept of "tools" has to exist in the first place.

The author is careful to note this is a comprehension aid, not a precise technical definition — Claude Code actually runs on top of Node.js and is, at heart, a user-space program.

qa.q: 书中提到"Token"，这是什么？为什么 Token 这么重要？
qa.qEn: The book talks about "tokens." What are they, and why do they matter so much?
qa.a:
Token 是 AI 模型处理文字的基本单位，类似于把文章切成碎片来处理。大约 1,000 个 Token 相当于 750 个英文单词，或者 500-600 个中文字——差不多一页 A4 纸的内容。

Token 重要是因为两点：第一，每个 AI 模型都有"上下文窗口"——它在一次对话里能"看到"的最大 Token 量，超过了就看不到更早的内容。Claude 的上下文窗口约 20 万 Token，听起来很大，但一旦对话很长、读了很多文件，很快就会填满。第二，Token 直接对应费用——每发送 1,000 个 Token 给 Claude，要付钱；而且输入 Token 和输出 Token 价格差很多，输出比输入贵约 5 倍。

Claude Code 的大量设计——六层上下文压缩机制、提示词缓存策略、工具结果大小限制——都是在精打细算地管理 Token，既避免超出上限，又尽量降低成本。书中把 Token 比作"货币"，缓存命中率比作"汇率"，非常传神。
qa.aEn:
A token is the basic unit an AI model uses to process text — essentially the model chops a passage into small pieces and handles them one by one. Roughly 1,000 tokens equals 750 English words, or 500-600 Chinese characters — about one page of A4 paper.

Tokens matter for two reasons. First, every AI model has a **context window** — the maximum number of tokens it can "see" in one conversation. Go past that limit and earlier content falls out of view. Claude's context window is about 200,000 tokens, which sounds huge, but once a conversation gets long and you've read in many files, it fills up fast. Second, tokens map directly to cost — you pay per thousand tokens sent to Claude, and input tokens and output tokens are priced very differently (output is about 5x more expensive than input).

A huge chunk of Claude Code's design — the six-layer context compression mechanism, the prompt-cache strategy, the tool-result size caps — is all about managing tokens carefully: staying under the limit and keeping cost down. The book's metaphor of tokens as *currency* and cache hit rate as *exchange rate* captures the spirit well.

qa.q: 什么是"社区公开流传的源码"？这本书的分析基础是什么，可靠吗？
qa.qEn: What does "community-released source code" mean here? What is the book's analysis based on, and is it reliable?
qa.a:
这本书分析的是 Claude Code 2.1.88 版本的源代码。这份代码是 2026 年 3 月通过 `cli.js.map` 在社区公开流传开来的，而非 Anthropic 官方公开发布的。

这是否可靠？作者的态度是：所有结论都有源文件路径和行号做支撑，不猜测、不臆断。你可以验证——每个论断都指向具体代码位置。这是源码级架构分析的标准做法：用证据说话，而非靠推测。

另一面是：代码会演进，书中引用的行号在你读到的时候可能已经变化了。但作者认为这没关系——代码的具体实现会变，但架构设计思路和工程权衡的逻辑寿命更长，这才是书想传递的核心价值。

附带一提：这次源码公开实际上催生了一场开源生态的爆发，大量开发者基于这份代码做出了衍生工具和分析，相互印证了代码的真实性。
qa.aEn:
The book analyzes the source code of Claude Code version 2.1.88. That code reached the public in March 2026 via `cli.js.map` circulated through the community — it was not released publicly by Anthropic.

Is it reliable? The author's stance: every conclusion is backed by a source file path and line number. No guessing, no hand-waving. You can verify — every claim points at a specific location in the code. This is standard practice for source-level architecture analysis: let the evidence do the talking.

The flip side: code evolves. By the time you read this, the line numbers cited in the book may already have shifted. The author's view is that this doesn't really matter — concrete implementations change, but the logic behind architectural decisions and engineering trade-offs has a much longer shelf life, and that's the value the book is trying to transmit.

One side note: this source release actually triggered an open-source ecosystem explosion. A wave of developers built derivative tools and analyses on top of this code, and they cross-corroborate each other, which is itself strong evidence that the code is genuine.

qa.q: 书里提到的"Harness 工程"是什么意思？和普通写 AI 程序有什么区别？
qa.qEn: What does the book mean by "Harness engineering"? How is it different from ordinary AI-application development?
qa.a:
行业里有一个被广泛接受的三层框架：Model 层（底层大模型，负责"思考"）、Runtime 层（调用编排和上下文管理，负责"传递"）、Harness 层（面向用户的完整产品体验，负责"感知和执行"）。

最简单的 AI 应用只是"调 API 拿回答"——这停留在 Runtime 层。Harness 工程则是全面设计 AI 和真实世界交互的"车架"——包括：AI 能做什么、怎么和用户沟通、权限如何控制、状态怎么管理、出错怎么恢复。

打个比方：同样是汽车发动机（Model），装在跑车、卡车还是救护车上（Harness），使用体验完全不同。Claude Code、Cursor、GitHub Copilot 背后可能调用的是同一个 Claude 模型，但各自的"车架"设计完全不同——这才决定了产品差异。本书解剖的正是 Claude Code 这辆车的"车架"怎么造的。
qa.aEn:
The industry has largely converged on a three-layer framework: the **Model** layer (the underlying large language model that handles "thinking"), the **Runtime** layer (call orchestration and context management that handles "transport"), and the **Harness** layer (the full user-facing product experience that handles "perception and action").

The simplest AI applications just "call the API and return a response" — they stop at the Runtime layer. Harness engineering is about designing the entire *chassis* of how an AI interacts with the real world: what the AI is allowed to do, how it talks to the user, how permissions are controlled, how state is managed, how errors are recovered.

Put another way: the same engine (Model) behaves very differently depending on whether you drop it into a sports car, a truck, or an ambulance (Harness). Claude Code, Cursor, and GitHub Copilot may all call the same Claude model under the hood, but their chassis designs are completely different — and that's what drives the product differences. What this book dissects is how Claude Code's chassis is built.

qa.q: 如果 Claude Code 选择做成 VS Code 插件或网页版，会有什么不同？为什么它选择纯终端？
qa.qEn: What would be different if Claude Code had been built as a VS Code extension or a web app? Why did it go pure terminal?
qa.a:
这是一个有意思的设计选择题。做成 VS Code 插件的好处是：文件系统访问、UI 渲染、进程管理这些问题 VS Code 都帮你解决了，你只需要专注 AI 逻辑。做成网页版则更易于分发，不需要用户安装任何东西。

但纯终端带来了独特的优势：可以在没有图形界面的远程服务器上工作（很多工程师的主要工作环境）；可以和任何编辑器（Vim、Emacs、Neovim）无缝集成；可以完全通过键盘操作，适合自动化流水线。这些场景是 IDE 插件和网页版做不到的。

代价是：在 IDE 环境里"免费"获得的东西——UI 渲染、文件版本追踪、进程隔离——Claude Code 得自己从头造。这就是为什么它有 Ink/React 终端渲染框架、自己的文件历史快照系统、自己的沙箱进程隔离。选择纯终端，意味着选择了更高的工程复杂度，换来了更强的平台无关性和自动化适配性。
qa.aEn:
This is a genuinely interesting design question. A VS Code extension has obvious upsides: filesystem access, UI rendering, and process management are all handled by VS Code, so you just focus on the AI logic. A web app is even easier to distribute — no install required.

But a pure terminal app brings unique advantages: it works on remote servers with no GUI (the main working environment for many engineers); it integrates seamlessly with any editor (Vim, Emacs, Neovim); and it's fully keyboard-driven, which is ideal for automation pipelines. IDE extensions and web apps can't cover these scenarios.

The cost: everything you get "for free" inside an IDE — UI rendering, file version tracking, process isolation — Claude Code has to build from scratch. That's why it ships with an Ink/React terminal renderer, its own file-history snapshot system, and its own sandboxed process isolation. Going pure terminal means accepting higher engineering complexity in exchange for stronger platform neutrality and automation-friendliness.

qa.q: 书中说"比喻不是降低精度，是提供另一个入口"——这句话怎么理解？
qa.qEn: The book says "metaphor doesn't reduce precision, it gives you another way in." How should I read that line?
qa.a:
这反映了作者的一个写作哲学，也值得认真对待。

很多技术书为了"严谨"，直接上术语和公式，结果读者根本建立不起直觉。而另一种极端是把所有东西都类比化，却丢失了关键的精确性。

这本书的做法是两步走：先用类比建立直觉（比如把权限系统比作"小区门禁"），让你大脑里有一个可以挂靠的形象；然后用源代码和具体机制来校准这个直觉——如果类比有偏差，明确指出"这里类比不成立，因为……"。

这个方法的价值在于：类比是可操作的思维工具。当你在脑子里装着"权限系统=门禁"，遇到新问题时你会本能地想"这在门禁里对应什么"，这往往比死记硬背规则更有效。精度是通过"比喻 + 校准"共同实现的，而不是靠牺牲理解的易得性来换取的。
qa.aEn:
This reflects the author's writing philosophy, and it deserves to be taken seriously.

Many technical books, in the name of "rigor," jump straight to jargon and formulas — and readers never build any intuition at all. The opposite extreme is to metaphor-ize everything and lose the crucial precision along the way.

This book uses a two-step approach: first, use a metaphor to build intuition (e.g., comparing the permission system to an apartment-complex security gate), giving you a mental image to hang new concepts on. Then, use the source code and concrete mechanisms to *calibrate* that intuition — whenever the metaphor diverges from reality, the book says explicitly, "the analogy breaks down here, because..."

The value of this method: metaphors are operable thinking tools. If your head carries "permission system = security gate," when you hit a new problem your instinct is to ask "what corresponds to this in the security-gate picture?" — which is often far more effective than memorizing rules. Precision emerges from *metaphor + calibration* together, not by sacrificing approachability.

qa.q: 一次源码公开怎么会催生一整个开源生态？这是偶然还是必然？
qa.qEn: How did a single source-code release spawn an entire open-source ecosystem? Was it accident or inevitability?
qa.a:
从结果来看，催生效应相当惊人：源码公开一周内 GitHub 上涌现了 6 个以上的 CC 相关热门项目，累计获得数万星标，甚至出现了专门的可视化导览网站和系统性框架书。

为什么会这样？有几个深层原因。第一，Claude Code 是一个工业级的、可研究的 AI Agent "参考实现"——在此之前，行业缺乏一个成熟系统的完整代码。这份代码填补了这个空白，对希望构建类似系统的开发者来说价值极高。第二，AI Agent 架构正处于快速演进期，整个行业急需高质量的工程案例来形成共识——源码公开恰好在这个时机发生。第三，代码质量确实高，设计决策经得起分析。

说它是"必然"也许夸大了，但充分条件确实成立：一份高质量参考代码 + 行业需求旺盛 + 恰当的时机 = 生态爆发。这反过来也说明了一件事：在 AI 软件工程领域，好的架构设计本身就有传播价值。
qa.aEn:
The outcome was striking: within a week of the source release, more than six CC-related projects trended on GitHub, collectively pulling in tens of thousands of stars. Dedicated visualization walkthroughs and full framework books emerged.

Why did this happen? A few deeper reasons. First, Claude Code is an industrial-grade, researchable **reference implementation** of an AI Agent — and before this, the industry had no complete codebase of a mature system to study. This code filled that gap, and it's enormously valuable to anyone trying to build something similar. Second, AI Agent architecture is evolving rapidly and the field badly needs high-quality engineering case studies to converge on shared standards — the source release happened to land exactly at that moment. Third, the code quality genuinely is high; the design decisions hold up under analysis.

Calling it "inevitable" may be too strong, but the sufficient conditions were all in place: a high-quality reference codebase + strong industry demand + the right moment = ecosystem explosion. This also says something more general: in AI software engineering, good architectural design has propagation value in its own right.

qa.q: 操作系统类比真的成立吗？硬用这个比喻会不会误导读者？
qa.qEn: Does the OS analogy actually hold up? Could forcing this metaphor mislead readers?
qa.a:
这是一个值得认真对待的质疑。作者自己也承认"'操作系统'是一个帮助理解的类比，不是精确的技术定义"，Claude Code 运行在 Node.js 之上，本质是用户态应用程序，不具备真正 OS 的硬件控制能力和特权级隔离。

类比真正失效的地方：真正的 OS 内核运行在特权模式（Ring 0），有直接的硬件访问权；Claude Code 的所有操作都受宿主 OS 约束，它的"沙箱"是应用层的，不是硬件级的。另外，OS 的进程调度是抢占式的（OS 强行切换进程），Claude Code 的 Agent 调度是协作式的（Agent 自己决定何时让出控制权）。

但类比成立的地方也是真实的：职责高度同构（资源调度、安全隔离、生命周期管理、扩展机制）。如果你把这个类比当作"功能职责的对应"而非"实现机制的等同"，它是有效的思维工具。只要读者意识到这个边界，类比就不会误导，而是提供真实帮助。
qa.aEn:
This challenge deserves to be taken seriously. The author openly admits the "operating system" framing is a comprehension aid, not a precise technical definition — Claude Code runs on top of Node.js, is fundamentally a user-space application, and has neither the hardware-control capabilities nor the privilege-level isolation of a real OS.

Where the analogy genuinely breaks: a real OS kernel runs in privileged mode (Ring 0) with direct hardware access. Every operation Claude Code performs is constrained by the host OS; its "sandbox" lives at the application layer, not at the hardware level. Also, OS process scheduling is preemptive (the OS forcibly switches processes), while Claude Code's agent scheduling is cooperative (the agent itself decides when to yield).

But where the analogy holds is also real: the responsibilities are highly isomorphic (resource scheduling, security isolation, lifecycle management, extension mechanisms). If you treat the analogy as "correspondence of functional responsibilities" rather than "equivalence of implementation mechanisms," it's an effective thinking tool. As long as readers are aware of the boundary, the analogy doesn't mislead — it genuinely helps.

qa.q: 分析社区公开的源码是否涉及法律或道德问题？这本书怎么处理这个问题？
qa.qEn: Are there legal or ethical issues with analyzing community-released source code? How does the book handle them?
qa.a:
这是一个诚实的问题，不应该回避。

从法律角度：使用未经官方授权的源码存在潜在的版权风险——代码的著作权属于 Anthropic，未经授权分发或使用可能违反版权法，具体判断取决于所在地区的法律和使用方式。

从道德角度：作者在书的声明部分明确表达了对 Anthropic 工程团队的"真诚敬意"，批评也是工程分析应有的态度，不是为了贬低。这种姿态是可取的——分析优秀工程的目的是学习，而非损害原作者利益。

需要注意的是：本书的定位是教育性的工程分析，类似于学术界的"源码级架构分析论文"。这类工作有先例（如对 Linux 内核、JVM 实现的学术分析），通常被视为合理使用的边界区域。但这不是法律建议，读者和作者都应对自己的使用方式负责。对这个问题保持清醒意识，比假装它不存在更负责任。
qa.aEn:
This is an honest question and shouldn't be dodged.

On the legal side: using source code without official authorization carries real copyright risk — the code is copyrighted by Anthropic, and unauthorized redistribution or use may violate copyright law, with the specifics depending on your jurisdiction and how you use it.

On the ethical side: the book's preface openly expresses "sincere respect" for the Anthropic engineering team. Critique is what a proper engineering analysis requires — it isn't meant to disparage. This stance is appropriate: the purpose of analyzing excellent engineering is to learn from it, not to harm the original creator's interests.

Worth noting: this book is positioned as educational engineering analysis, similar in spirit to academic source-level architecture analysis papers. Such work has precedent (academic analyses of the Linux kernel, JVM implementations), and usually sits in a grey zone generally considered fair use. But this is not legal advice — readers and authors are each responsible for their own use. Keeping clear-eyed awareness of this question is more responsible than pretending it doesn't exist.

qa.q: 文中展示了"模型 A（错误的）"和"模型 B（实际的）"，为什么大多数人对 Claude Code 的理解是错的？
qa.qEn: The chapter shows a "Model A (wrong)" and "Model B (actual)" — why do most people get Claude Code wrong?
qa.a:
大多数人对 Claude Code 的理解停留在"用户输入 → AI 调用 → AI 输出"这个三步模型，这来自普通聊天机器人的使用经验。这个模型在 ChatGPT 的网页界面上大致没错，但放到 Claude Code 就完全错了。

实际上，当你按下 Enter 之后，系统开始了一个非常复杂的流水线：权限系统先检查你的请求是否被允许，上下文构建器开始组装 git 状态、项目说明文件、对话历史，然后主循环开始。主循环里，AI 可能连续调用十几个工具（读文件、搜索代码、执行命令），每次工具调用都需要权限检查，工具还可能并行执行。快完成时，后台有另一个 AI 在悄悄更新你的项目笔记，还有一个 AI 在预测你下一句话并提前开始计算。

这不是"一个 API 调用"——这是一个有自己生命周期的、多 AI 协作的、流水线执行的工程系统。理解这个区别，你才能理解为什么 Claude Code 的代码有 1,884 个文件，而一个简单聊天机器人几百行代码就够了。
qa.aEn:
Most people's mental model of Claude Code stops at three steps — "user input → AI call → AI output" — carried over from experience with ordinary chatbots. That model is roughly correct for the ChatGPT web UI, but it's completely wrong for Claude Code.

In reality, the moment you hit Enter, the system kicks off a complex pipeline: the permission system checks whether your request is allowed; the context builder assembles git state, project instruction files, and conversation history; then the main loop starts. Inside the main loop, the AI may call a dozen tools in sequence (read files, search code, run commands), each call requires a permission check, and tools may execute in parallel. Near the end, another AI is quietly updating your project notes in the background, and yet another AI is predicting your next message and pre-computing a response.

This is not "one API call" — it's an engineering system with its own lifecycle, multi-agent coordination, and pipelined execution. Once you see the difference, you understand why Claude Code spans 1,884 files while a simple chatbot only takes a few hundred lines.

qa.q: Claude Code 里的"工具"是什么？AI 为什么不能直接读文件而必须通过工具？
qa.qEn: What are "tools" in Claude Code? Why can't the AI read files directly — why does it have to go through a tool?
qa.a:
在 Claude Code 里，"工具"是 AI 可以请求执行的一种能力。内置工具有 40 个目录，比如读文件（Read）、编辑文件（Edit）、执行命令（Bash）、创建子 Agent（Agent）、搜索网页（WebFetch）等。

为什么 AI 不能直接读文件？因为 Claude 模型运行在 Anthropic 的远程服务器上，它根本"触不到"你的电脑。当 Claude 说"我要读这个文件"，它实际上是输出了一段结构化文本，说"请帮我调用 Read 工具，参数是这个文件路径"。Claude Code 客户端（运行在你电脑上）接收这个请求，在本地执行，把结果发回给 Claude。

这个机制有一个重要的安全含义：工具调用经过权限检查。不是 Claude 想做什么就能做什么，每个工具调用都要过一道检查——是否在允许列表里？是否需要用户确认？这就是为什么 Claude Code 在执行敏感操作时会弹出确认框，而普通聊天 AI 不会。工具是 AI 与真实世界之间的唯一桥梁，也是安全控制的关键节点。
qa.aEn:
In Claude Code, a **tool** is a capability the AI can ask the system to execute. There are 40 built-in tool directories — Read (read file), Edit (edit file), Bash (run command), Agent (spawn sub-agent), WebFetch (fetch a web page), and so on.

Why can't the AI just read a file directly? Because the Claude model runs on Anthropic's remote servers — it literally can't touch your machine. When Claude says "I need to read this file," what it actually emits is a piece of structured text that says "please call the Read tool with this file path as the argument." The Claude Code client (running on your computer) receives that request, executes it locally, and sends the result back to Claude.

This mechanism has a crucial security implication: **tool calls go through permission checks**. Claude can't just do whatever it wants. Every tool call passes through a gate — is it on the allowlist? Does it need user confirmation? That's why Claude Code pops up a confirmation dialog for sensitive operations and a regular chat AI doesn't. Tools are the only bridge between the AI and the real world, and they're also the key control point for security.

qa.q: 书中说 Claude Code 有 8 个子系统，这些子系统是各自独立运行的吗？它们怎么协调？
qa.qEn: The book says Claude Code has 8 subsystems. Do they run independently? How do they coordinate?
qa.a:
这 8 个子系统（权限系统、上下文构建、工具执行、上下文压缩、Agent 系统、MCP 层、SessionMemory、Speculation）确实各自独立——每个子系统有自己的代码文件、自己的逻辑职责，互不干扰。

但它们通过两个机制协调：一是共享的全局状态（AppState），这是一个贯穿整个系统的状态对象，记录当前权限、运行中的任务、MCP 连接等，任何子系统都可以读取；二是 Hooks 系统——系统有 27 个事件节点，子系统可以在这些节点上"挂钩子"，当特定事件发生时自动触发。

打个比方：这 8 个子系统就像一家餐厅的不同部门（前台、后厨、收银、仓库……），各自有各自的工作，但通过共享的订单系统（AppState）知道整体状态，通过"叫号广播"（Hooks）在关键时刻互相通知。这种设计的好处是子系统可以独立开发和测试，坏处是调试时要追踪多个部分的联动——复杂性的代价。
qa.aEn:
The 8 subsystems (permissions, context building, tool execution, context compression, Agent system, MCP layer, SessionMemory, Speculation) are indeed independent — each has its own source files and its own logical responsibility, and they don't interfere with each other.

But they coordinate via two mechanisms. First, **shared global state (AppState)** — a state object threaded through the entire system that tracks current permissions, running tasks, MCP connections, and more; any subsystem can read it. Second, the **Hooks system** — there are 27 event points in the system, and subsystems can "hang hooks" on them to auto-fire when specific events occur.

Metaphor: the 8 subsystems are like the departments of a restaurant (front of house, kitchen, cashier, pantry...). Each has its own job, but they know the overall state through a shared ordering system (AppState), and they signal each other at key moments through a "number-calling PA system" (Hooks). The upside of this design: subsystems can be developed and tested independently. The downside: when debugging, you have to trace interactions across multiple parts — the tax of complexity.

qa.q: 为什么 Claude Code 要用 React 来渲染终端界面？这不是一个奇怪的技术选择吗？
qa.qEn: Why does Claude Code use React to render its terminal UI? Isn't that a weird technical choice?
qa.a:
确实听起来奇怪——React 不是用来做网页的吗？在终端里用 React 做界面，这不是用高射炮打蚊子吗？

背后有一个库叫 Ink，它把 React 的组件模型"翻译"给终端。你用 React 写 <Box><Text>hello</Text></Box>，Ink 帮你计算出它在终端里应该占哪些字符位置并渲染出来。

为什么值得这样做？因为 Claude Code 的终端界面远比看起来复杂：AI 在流式输出时，界面要实时更新；多个 Agent 并行运行时，要同时显示多个进度；用户输入框、错误提示、确认弹窗……这些 UI 元素需要协调管理状态。如果用传统的"打印字符串"方式来写，管理这种动态状态是噩梦。

React 的声明式组件模型（你描述 UI 应该长什么样，框架帮你更新）正好解决了这个问题。用 Ink + React 意味着整个团队可以复用 Web 开发的心智模型和工具链，降低了开发终端 UI 的认知成本。奇怪的选择，但有充分的工程理由。
qa.aEn:
It does sound weird — isn't React for web pages? Using React to paint a terminal UI feels like swatting a mosquito with an anti-aircraft gun, right?

The key is a library called **Ink**, which translates React's component model into terminal output. You write `<Box><Text>hello</Text></Box>` in React, and Ink figures out which character cells it should occupy in the terminal and renders accordingly.

Why is it worth the trouble? Because Claude Code's terminal UI is far more complex than it looks: the view has to update in real time while the AI streams output; multiple agents running in parallel need simultaneous progress indicators; input boxes, error banners, confirmation dialogs — these UI elements all need coordinated state management. If you wrote this with the traditional "just print strings" approach, managing that dynamic state would be a nightmare.

React's declarative component model (you describe what the UI should look like and the framework handles updates) is exactly the right fit. Using Ink + React means the whole team can reuse web-development mental models and tooling, which lowers the cognitive cost of building terminal UIs. A weird choice — but with solid engineering justification.

qa.q: "Speculation（投机执行）"是什么？在你还没打字的时候 AI 就开始工作，这不会浪费资源吗？
qa.qEn: What is Speculation (speculative execution)? If the AI starts working before you've finished typing, isn't that wasteful?
qa.a:
投机执行是一个非常有趣的优化：系统会预测你下一句话最可能是什么，然后提前用这个预测消息去跑一遍 AI 循环。如果你真的说了这句话，结果已经准备好了，响应速度大幅提升；如果你说了别的，预测结果就被丢弃。

这确实会浪费资源——如果预测命中率不高，大量 Token 和计算白白消耗。所以这个特性的价值完全取决于预测准确率。Claude Code 的预测通常基于上下文推断（比如你刚让 AI 修了一个 bug，下一步很可能是"运行测试"），在某些工作流里命中率相当高。

这个设计有一个很有意思的工程哲学：把"等待时间"变成"工作时间"。用户从发送消息到读取回复，中间有一段键盘输入时间——以前这段时间系统在空转，现在系统在提前计算。这是序章里提到的"在等待时间里藏工作"这条工程哲学的具体体现。资源消耗是代价，响应延迟的降低是收益，权衡成立与否取决于命中率。
qa.aEn:
Speculation is a genuinely fun optimization: the system predicts what your next message is most likely to be, then pre-runs the AI loop against that predicted message. If you actually say that thing, the result is ready — your response latency drops dramatically. If you say something else, the speculative result gets thrown away.

And yes, it does waste resources — if prediction accuracy is low, a lot of tokens and compute go straight in the bin. So the value of this feature depends entirely on prediction accuracy. Claude Code's predictions are usually grounded in contextual inference (e.g., you just asked the AI to fix a bug, so the next step is likely "run the tests"), and in some workflows the hit rate is quite high.

There's a nice engineering philosophy hiding here: **turn waiting time into working time**. Between sending a message and reading the reply, there's a keyboard-input gap — the system used to idle through that gap, now it computes ahead. This is a concrete manifestation of the Preface's engineering maxim "hide work inside waiting." Resource consumption is the cost, latency reduction is the benefit, and whether the trade-off works depends on hit rate.

qa.q: Claude Code 和 Aider、Cursor 这些竞品相比，哪个设计更好？
qa.qEn: How does Claude Code compare to competitors like Aider and Cursor — which design is better?
qa.a:
这是一个没有标准答案的问题，因为"更好"取决于使用场景和价值权衡。

Aider 的设计哲学是"简单但专注"——它用 Repo Map（提前只加载代码仓库中相关函数签名，而非整个文件）来预过滤上下文，而不是事后压缩；工具集小，没有多层子 Agent。这在小型代码仓库和标准化工作流里效率很高，入门门槛低。

Cursor 走了"IDE 原生"路线——深度集成在编辑器里，用户体验流畅，但牺牲了平台无关性和自动化适配能力。

Claude Code 选择了"完整性和灵活性"——40 个内置工具目录、6 种 Task 类型、6 层压缩、27 个 Hook 节点，这使它在复杂任务和需要高度定制化的场景下表现最好，但学习成本和资源消耗也最高。

一个公平的评价是：没有哪个设计在所有维度都赢。Claude Code 胜在复杂任务能力和系统完整性，输在上手难度和资源消耗。好设计不是"最好"，而是"最适合它要解决的问题"。
qa.aEn:
There's no single right answer, because "better" depends on use case and value trade-offs.

**Aider** follows a "simple but focused" philosophy — it uses a **Repo Map** (preloading only the relevant function signatures rather than full files) to pre-filter context, instead of compressing after the fact; its tool set is small, with no layered sub-agents. It's very efficient for small repos and standardized workflows, and has a low on-ramp.

**Cursor** takes the "IDE-native" route — deeply integrated into the editor, smooth user experience, but sacrifices platform independence and automation friendliness.

**Claude Code** chooses "completeness and flexibility" — 40 built-in tool directories, 6 Task types, 6 layers of compression, 27 Hook events. This lets it shine on complex tasks and heavily customized scenarios, but the learning cost and resource consumption are also the highest.

A fair verdict: no design wins on every axis. Claude Code wins on complex-task capability and system completeness; it loses on onboarding difficulty and resource consumption. Good design isn't "the best" — it's "the best fit for the problem it set out to solve."

qa.q: 一个工程系统有 1,884 个文件、主入口文件 785KB——这是过度工程化吗？
qa.qEn: An engineering system with 1,884 files and a 785KB main entry file — is this over-engineering?
qa.a:
这是一个值得认真讨论的批判，而不是可以轻松否认的质疑。

从某种角度看，这确实可能是过度工程化的迹象：785KB 的单文件入口（main.tsx）意味着某个层次的模块化没有做好；1,884 个文件的规模超过很多大型商业软件；很多功能（比如 Speculation、SessionMemory）是锦上添花的优化，并非核心功能必需。

但也有为这个复杂度辩护的理由：它解决的问题确实复杂——需要同时处理并发安全、权限控制、状态持久化、多平台兼容、企业级配置管理；它面向的用户群（专业开发者）接受更高的复杂度换取更强的能力；历史积累和功能叠加本来就会增加代码量。

书中在 Part 6 也会讨论"复杂度代价"的问题，不回避这个质疑。一个诚实的结论是：这个系统的复杂度有一部分是必要的（问题本身的复杂度），有一部分可能是历史债务（快速迭代的代价）。区分这两者，需要读者自己判断。
qa.aEn:
This is a critique worth engaging with seriously, not one to brush off.

From one angle, yes, these are plausible signs of over-engineering: a 785KB single-file entry (main.tsx) suggests modularization broke down somewhere; 1,884 files exceeds many large commercial software projects; several features (Speculation, SessionMemory) are nice-to-have optimizations, not requirements for core functionality.

But there's also a case to be made for the complexity: the problem is genuinely complex — it has to handle concurrency safety, permission control, state persistence, cross-platform compatibility, and enterprise-grade configuration management all at once; the target user base (professional developers) tolerates higher complexity in exchange for more power; historical accumulation and layered feature additions naturally grow the code size.

The book takes this critique head-on in Part 6's discussion of the "cost of complexity." An honest conclusion: part of this system's complexity is necessary (inherent to the problem), and part may be historical debt (the price of fast iteration). Telling them apart is something the reader has to decide for themselves.

qa.q: 系统架构图里有 ① 到 ⑥ 共六个步骤，一次普通对话真的每次都要走完所有步骤吗？
qa.qEn: The architecture diagram shows six steps (① to ⑥). Does every normal conversation actually go through all of them?
qa.a:
不是所有步骤每次都会触发，但主循环（步骤 ③）肯定会运行，而且会循环多次，不是一次性完成的。

具体来说：步骤 ① 和 ② 每次对话必走；步骤 ③ 的主循环会根据 AI 的行为决定循环几次——如果 AI 只给了一个简单回答、没有调用任何工具，可能只循环一次；如果 AI 需要读文件、执行命令、再基于结果进一步处理，可能循环 5-20 次；步骤 ④ 是主循环结束的标志；步骤 ⑤（SessionMemory 提取、Prompt Suggestion）是后台异步运行的，不影响主流程速度；步骤 ⑥（Speculation 投机执行）是可选的，需要特定条件才触发。

一个直觉：你越是提出需要多步操作的复杂任务，主循环循环的次数越多，整个流程越接近图里展示的完整状态。问"改这个文件的第三行"可能只需要 2-3 轮循环；问"重构这个模块的架构"可能需要几十轮。
qa.aEn:
Not every step fires every time, but the main loop (step ③) always runs — and it loops multiple times, not just once.

More concretely: steps ① and ② run on every conversation. Step ③'s main loop runs however many times the AI's behavior demands — if the AI gives a simple answer with no tool calls, it might loop once; if the AI needs to read files, run commands, and process results further, it might loop 5-20 times. Step ④ marks the end of the main loop. Step ⑤ (SessionMemory extraction, Prompt Suggestion) runs asynchronously in the background and doesn't slow the main flow. Step ⑥ (Speculation) is optional and only fires under specific conditions.

Rule of thumb: the more multi-step your request, the more loops the main loop takes, and the closer the actual flow gets to the full diagram. "Change line 3 of this file" might need 2-3 loops; "refactor this module's architecture" might need dozens.

qa.q: "StreamingToolExecutor（流式工具执行器）"和普通的工具执行有什么区别？为什么要这样设计？
qa.qEn: How is StreamingToolExecutor different from ordinary tool execution? Why design it this way?
qa.a:
普通（传统）路径是：等 AI 把这一轮所有话说完 → 拿到所有工具调用请求 → 然后按批次执行工具。AI 说话期间，工具系统在等待。

流式路径（StreamingToolExecutor）是：AI 每说出一个完整的工具调用请求 → 立刻开始执行，不等 AI 说完。这意味着当 AI 刚说完"第三个工具请求"的时候，前两个工具的执行可能已经完成了。

为什么要这样？核心目标是降低用户等待时间。AI 生成文字本来就需要时间（尤其是当上下文很长时），如果在这段时间里什么都不干，用户就要白白等两份时间：等 AI 说完 + 等工具执行完。流式执行把这两段时间"叠加"——AI 在说话，工具在执行，用户只等最长的那一段。

这个设计有个前提假设：AI 说出的工具调用彼此独立（A 工具的执行不依赖 B 工具的结果）。如果 AI 的逻辑是"先读文件再基于内容写文件"，那前一个工具执行完才能确定第二个工具的参数，流式优化就不适用了。
qa.aEn:
The traditional path: wait for the AI to finish its full turn → collect all tool-call requests → execute them in a batch. While the AI is talking, the tool system is idling.

The streaming path (StreamingToolExecutor): the instant the AI emits one complete tool-call request → start executing it, don't wait for the rest of the turn. That means by the time the AI has finished emitting the third tool request, the first two tool calls may already have completed.

Why do it this way? The core goal is to reduce user wait time. Generating text already takes the AI meaningful time (especially with a long context), and if the system does nothing during that window, the user effectively pays two waits: wait for the AI to finish + wait for tools to execute. Streaming execution **overlaps** these two waits — the AI talks while tools run, and the user only waits for the longer of the two.

This design rests on one assumption: the tool calls the AI emits are independent of each other (tool A's execution doesn't depend on tool B's result). If the AI's logic is "read a file, then write a new file based on its contents," the second tool's parameters can't be known until the first finishes, and the streaming optimization doesn't apply.

qa.q: 章节里提到"SystemPrompt（系统提示词）"，它和普通的对话消息有什么区别？它的内容是什么？
qa.qEn: The chapter mentions the SystemPrompt — how is it different from normal conversation messages, and what does it contain?
qa.a:
SystemPrompt 是每次 API 调用时发送给 Claude 的"背景信息包"，在用户消息之前发送，Claude 把它理解为"工作环境说明"，而不是正常的对话内容。

SystemPrompt 包含了很多内容：Claude 扮演什么角色、它被允许使用哪些工具（工具的名称和描述）、项目说明文件（CLAUDE.md）的内容、今天的日期和系统信息、git 仓库的当前状态、用户自定义的行为规则……这些是每次对话都要重复发送的"固定背景"。

这带来一个重要的成本问题：SystemPrompt 有 15,000 到 34,000 个 Token 那么长。不管你说什么，每次 API 调用都要支付这笔"固定税"。这也是为什么系统要精心设计 Prompt 缓存策略——把 SystemPrompt 这个不变的部分缓存起来，后续请求可以用缓存版本（费用只有原来的十分之一），而不是每次都重新处理。所以 SystemPrompt 的内容顺序很重要：最稳定的内容放最前面，最容易变化的（比如当前 git 状态）放最后面，确保最长的不变前缀能被缓存命中。
qa.aEn:
The SystemPrompt is the "background-information packet" sent to Claude on every API call, delivered before the user's message. Claude interprets it as a "description of the working environment" rather than as normal conversational content.

The SystemPrompt contains a lot: the role Claude is supposed to play, the tools it's allowed to use (their names and descriptions), the contents of the project instruction file (CLAUDE.md), today's date and system information, the current state of the git repo, any user-defined behavior rules. This is fixed background that gets re-sent on every conversation turn.

This creates a significant cost problem: the SystemPrompt is between 15,000 and 34,000 tokens long. No matter what you say, every API call has to pay this "fixed tax." That's why the system painstakingly designs its **Prompt Cache** strategy — cache the unchanging SystemPrompt so subsequent requests can hit the cached version (at about one-tenth the normal price) instead of reprocessing from scratch. Order matters: the most stable content goes at the front, the most variable content (current git state) goes at the back, ensuring the longest possible unchanged prefix can hit the cache.

qa.q: 文中的"权限三层控制"有什么实际意义？在日常使用中我会感受到哪一层？
qa.qEn: What's the practical point of the three-layer permission system? Which layer actually shows up in everyday use?
qa.a:
三层权限控制从静态到动态、从自动到手动：第一层是规则（你在配置文件或 CLAUDE.md 里预先写好"这类操作永远允许/永远拒绝"），第二层是工具自检（每个工具运行时检查当前操作的具体参数是否安全），第三层是用户确认（如果前两层没有明确答案，弹出对话框让你决定）。

日常使用中，绝大多数用户最直接感受到的是第三层——那个弹出来的"Claude 想要执行 XX 操作，是否允许？"确认框。这就是第三层在说"我不确定这个操作该不该做，你来判断"。

第一层对高级用户更重要——你可以在 CLAUDE.md 里写"允许 Claude 不问我直接执行 npm test"，让测试命令跳过确认。第二层对所有人都在默默工作——比如 Claude 不会在未经你允许的情况下在 .git/ 这种敏感目录里瞎改文件，这是工具自检的第二层保护在起作用，而你感受不到它的存在。

这种多层设计的价值是：单一层次的安全机制往往要在"方便"和"安全"之间二选一，多层机制允许不同场景选用不同层次，更灵活。
qa.aEn:
The three layers move from static to dynamic, from automatic to manual. **Layer 1** is rules — you pre-declare in config files or CLAUDE.md that "this class of operation is always allowed / always denied." **Layer 2** is tool self-check — each tool inspects the specific arguments of the current operation at runtime to see whether they're safe. **Layer 3** is user confirmation — if the first two layers don't reach a clear answer, a dialog pops up and you decide.

In daily use, the layer most users feel directly is **Layer 3** — the pop-up that asks "Claude wants to run XX, allow it?" That's Layer 3 saying "I'm not sure whether this should happen; you call it."

**Layer 1** matters more to power users — you can put "allow Claude to run npm test without asking" in CLAUDE.md to let test commands bypass confirmation. **Layer 2** quietly works for everyone — for example, Claude won't mess with files in a sensitive directory like `.git/` without your permission, thanks to tool self-check, and you never notice it's there.

The value of multi-layer design: a single-layer security mechanism usually has to pick between "convenient" and "safe." Multi-layer lets different scenarios pick different layers, which is far more flexible.

qa.q: 章节末尾说"五分钟的概览省略了大量边界情况"，最关键的被省略的部分是什么？
qa.qEn: The chapter ends by saying "this five-minute overview skips many edge cases." What are the most important ones it omits?
qa.a:
作者诚实地指出了三个最重要的省略：

第一，流式工具执行在网络不稳定时的回退策略。流式执行听起来很美，但如果 AI 说到一半网络断了、工具已经开始执行了怎么办？这种异常情况的处理逻辑比正常流程复杂得多，而且直接关系到系统可靠性。

第二，上下文压缩可能丢失关键信息。压缩机制确实很聪明，但它毕竟是在丢弃信息，只保留摘要。如果某个关键的上下文细节在压缩时被判为"不重要"并丢弃，Claude 后续的回答可能基于不完整的信息。这是一个功能和可靠性之间的真实权衡，没有完美解。

第三，Auto 模式下 AI 分类器可能做出错误的权限判断。在自动模式下，Claude Code 用另一个 AI 来代替人工判断"这个操作是否应该被允许"。AI 分类器会犯错，可能放行了应该拒绝的操作，或者拒绝了应该允许的操作。这个风险在批量自动化场景下尤为值得注意。

这三个问题都不是小问题，读懂它们需要继续深入后续章节。
qa.aEn:
The author honestly calls out three of the most important omissions.

**First, the fallback strategy for streaming tool execution under flaky networks.** Streaming execution sounds great, but what happens if the network drops mid-turn while a tool is already running? The error-handling logic here is much more complex than the happy path, and it directly affects system reliability.

**Second, context compression can drop critical information.** The compression mechanism is clever, but it's still discarding information and keeping only summaries. If some key contextual detail gets judged "unimportant" and thrown away during compression, Claude's subsequent answers may rest on incomplete information. This is a real trade-off between capability and reliability with no perfect solution.

**Third, in Auto mode the AI classifier can make bad permission decisions.** In Auto mode, Claude Code uses another AI to decide, in place of a human, "should this operation be allowed?" AI classifiers make mistakes — they may let through operations that should have been denied, or deny operations that should have been allowed. This risk is especially worth watching in batch automation scenarios.

None of these are small issues. Grasping them properly requires reading deeper into later chapters.

qa.q: Model + Runtime + Harness 三层框架里，哪一层最重要？Claude Code 做的主要是哪一层？
qa.qEn: Of the Model + Runtime + Harness three-layer framework, which layer matters most? Which layer is Claude Code mostly about?
qa.a:
这个问题本身是个陷阱——"最重要"取决于你的目标。对于 AI 能力来说，Model 层（底层大模型）最重要；对于系统可靠性和产品体验来说，Harness 层往往是决定性因素。

Claude Code 的核心工作在 Harness 层。章节里提到的七个核心抽象（Tool、ToolUseContext、AppState、QueryEngine、Task、Command、SystemPrompt）几乎全部落在 Harness 层。换句话说，你在 Claude Code 里感受到的一切——工具系统、权限机制、界面渲染、状态管理——都是 Harness 层的工作。

Model 层（Claude 模型本身）和 Runtime 层（API 调用、流式传输）在这个框架里只是"管道"，Claude Code 并不控制它们。这带来一个有趣的推论：如果 Anthropic 换了底层模型，Claude Code 的整个 Harness 层代码基本不需要改动；反过来，如果只换 Harness 层而保留同一个 Claude 模型，用户体验可能变化巨大。这正是 Cursor 和 Claude Code 都用 Claude 模型、但体验差异明显的原因。
qa.aEn:
The question is a bit of a trap — "most important" depends on what you're optimizing for. For **AI capability**, the Model layer (the underlying LLM) matters most. For **system reliability and product experience**, the Harness layer is usually decisive.

Claude Code's core work lives in the Harness layer. The seven core abstractions the chapter calls out (Tool, ToolUseContext, AppState, QueryEngine, Task, Command, SystemPrompt) almost all sit at the Harness layer. In other words, everything you experience in Claude Code — the tool system, permission mechanism, UI rendering, state management — is Harness-layer work.

The Model layer (the Claude model itself) and the Runtime layer (API calls, streaming) are just "plumbing" in this framework; Claude Code doesn't control them. An interesting corollary: if Anthropic swaps the underlying model, Claude Code's entire Harness layer basically needs no changes; conversely, swap just the Harness layer while keeping the same Claude model and the user experience can be night-and-day different. That's exactly why Cursor and Claude Code both call the Claude model but feel strikingly different.

qa.q: 七个核心抽象里，哪一个是理解整个系统最关键的？如果只能精通一个该选哪个？
qa.qEn: Of the seven core abstractions, which one is the most important to understand? If I could only master one, which should it be?
qa.a:
这个问题有明确答案：QueryEngine 和它驱动的 queryLoop（主循环）是理解整个系统最关键的抽象。

原因是：主循环是系统的"心脏"——所有其他子系统都是围绕它工作的，或者被它调用，或者在它的生命周期内的某个节点注入逻辑。理解了主循环的运行机制（while(true) 的 AsyncGenerator、每轮调用 API、处理工具结果、检查退出条件），你就理解了系统的中枢神经。

另一个候选是 Tool 接口——它是系统所有"能力"的统一接口定义，理解了它等于理解了系统的扩展点在哪里。但 Tool 是"叶子节点"，QueryEngine/queryLoop 是"主干"。先理解主干，才能理解叶子的位置。

AppState 是第三个值得关注的——因为它是整个系统状态的唯一来源（Single Source of Truth），任何时刻系统的完整状态都在这里。但它的作用是"存储"而非"执行"，是辅助性的。所以学习顺序建议是：queryLoop → Tool → AppState，三者组合才能真正理解系统的骨架。
qa.aEn:
This one has a clear answer: **QueryEngine and the queryLoop (main loop) it drives** is the most important abstraction for understanding the whole system.

Here's why: the main loop is the system's **heart** — every other subsystem either orbits around it, is invoked by it, or injects logic at some point in its lifecycle. Once you understand how the main loop actually runs (`while(true)` AsyncGenerator, API call per turn, tool-result handling, exit-condition check), you understand the central nervous system.

The runner-up is the **Tool** interface — it's the unified interface definition for every "capability" in the system, and understanding it is equivalent to understanding where the extension points are. But Tool is a "leaf node"; QueryEngine/queryLoop is the "trunk." Understand the trunk first, then you know where the leaves belong.

**AppState** is worth calling out as the third — it's the **Single Source of Truth** for system state, and the complete state of the system at any instant lives there. But its role is "storage," not "execution" — auxiliary. Suggested learning order: queryLoop → Tool → AppState. All three together give you a genuine grasp of the system's skeleton.

qa.q: 上下文压缩的六层机制里，会不会存在压缩出错、导致 Claude 忘记重要信息的风险？系统有没有保护措施？
qa.qEn: Can the six-layer context-compression mechanism go wrong and cause Claude to forget important information? Does the system have safeguards?
qa.a:
这个风险是真实存在的，系统的设计没有完全消除它，而是接受了这个权衡。

从机制上说：前三层压缩（工具结果裁剪、历史片段剪裁、微压缩）是相对安全的——它们主要丢弃冗余内容（超长工具输出的末尾、重复的确认消息）；但第四层（上下文折叠，把三个月的对话压缩成摘要）和第五层（全文自动压缩）确实会造成信息损失，因为摘要本质上是有损压缩。

系统采取了一些保护措施：压缩是逐步的（先用轻量级方式，迫不得已才用重量级）；会保留最近的对话轮次（最近的内容通常最相关）；用另一个 Claude 实例来做摘要（而不是简单截断），尽量保留语义。

但根本性的保护措施是不存在的——如果信息真的被压缩掉了，Claude 就会"忘记"它。这是上下文窗口作为稀缺资源的根本限制，不是 Claude Code 能完全解决的问题。这也是为什么 CLAUDE.md 很重要——把最关键的项目信息写进 CLAUDE.md，它会被每次重新加载进上下文，不受压缩影响。
qa.aEn:
The risk is real. The system's design doesn't eliminate it — it accepts the trade-off.

Mechanically: the first three compression layers (tool-result trimming, history-fragment pruning, micro-compression) are relatively safe — they mostly drop redundant content (the tail of an overlong tool output, duplicate confirmation messages). But the fourth layer (context folding — compressing three months of conversation into a summary) and the fifth layer (full auto-compression) do cause information loss, because summarization is fundamentally lossy.

The system takes some safeguards: compression is progressive (lightweight methods first, heavyweight only when forced); the most recent turns are preserved (recent content is usually most relevant); summaries are produced by another Claude instance (rather than by simple truncation) to retain as much meaning as possible.

But there's no fundamental safeguard — if information truly gets compressed away, Claude will "forget" it. This is a basic limit of the context window as a scarce resource, not something Claude Code can fully solve. That's why **CLAUDE.md matters**: if you put the most critical project information in CLAUDE.md, it gets reloaded into context every time and is immune to compression.

chapter: part1/03_读懂本书需要的全部概念
title: 读懂本书需要的全部概念

qa.q: 本章介绍了很多概念，有没有一个"必须记住"的最短清单？哪些是高频出现的核心词汇？
qa.qEn: This chapter introduces a lot of concepts. Is there a short "must-remember" list? Which terms show up most often?
qa.a:
如果必须只记五个，按重要性排序是：Tool（工具）、Query Loop（主循环）、Agent（智能体）、Context Window（上下文窗口）、Permission（权限）。

Tool 是系统的基本能力单元——读文件、改代码、执行命令全靠它；Query Loop 是系统的心脏——所有 AI 推理和工具执行都在这里发生；Agent 是最容易和"Claude"混淆的概念——它指的是一个独立运行的 AI 实例，不是Claude模型本身；Context Window 是你和 Claude 对话的物理限制——超过了就要压缩；Permission 是安全防线——决定 Claude 能做什么、不能做什么。

次优先级（读到相关章节时查阅即可）：Session、SystemPrompt、CLAUDE.md、Hook、MCP、Sandbox、Prompt Cache、Settings。

不需要优先记忆的：代码内部代号（tengu、tungsten、bagel 等），这些只在阅读具体代码细节时有用。读书过程中遇到不认识的词，回到本章的"常见混淆辨析"和"快速检索索引"查就够了，不需要一次全部背下来。
qa.aEn:
If you had to keep only five, in order of importance: **Tool**, **Query Loop**, **Agent**, **Context Window**, **Permission**.

**Tool** is the system's basic capability unit — reading files, editing code, running commands all depend on it. **Query Loop** is the system's heart — all AI reasoning and tool execution happen inside it. **Agent** is the concept most easily confused with "Claude" — it refers to an independently running AI instance, not the Claude model itself. **Context Window** is the physical limit of your conversation with Claude — once exceeded, compression kicks in. **Permission** is the security line — deciding what Claude can and can't do.

Second-tier (look up when you hit the relevant chapter): Session, SystemPrompt, CLAUDE.md, Hook, MCP, Sandbox, Prompt Cache, Settings.

Don't prioritize memorizing internal code names (tengu, tungsten, bagel, etc.) — these only help when you're reading specific implementation details. When you hit an unfamiliar term while reading, come back to this chapter's "Common Confusions" and "Quick Reference Index" — you don't need to memorize everything up front.

qa.q: Session（会话）和 Context Window（上下文窗口）有什么区别？这两个词为什么容易混淆？
qa.qEn: What's the difference between Session and Context Window? Why are they so easy to confuse?
qa.a:
混淆的根源是：两者都和"对话的范围"有关，但它们处于不同的层次。

Session 存在于磁盘上——它是你整个对话历史的完整记录，以 JSONL 文件保存在 ~/.claude/projects/ 目录里，理论上可以无限长（只受磁盘空间限制），关掉终端后还在，可以恢复、回滚、分支。

Context Window 存在于内存里——它是每次 API 调用时塞进请求里的那部分对话，受 Claude 模型的硬限制（约 20 万 Token），每次 API 调用时重新组装，调用结束就消失。

关系是：Session 可能比 Context Window 大得多。当 Session 太长时，系统要从 Session 历史里"抽取"最相关的部分放进 Context Window。这就是六层压缩机制存在的原因——不是真正"压缩"了 Session（Session 文件始终完整保存），而是在 Context Window 这个有限窗口里塞入尽可能多的有效信息。

类比：Session 是你的全本日记（存在书架上），Context Window 是你随身带着的今天的便签本（只放最相关的内容）。带着便签本去开会，不是因为日记丢了，而是因为日记太厚带不走。
qa.aEn:
The source of the confusion: both relate to "the scope of a conversation," but they sit at different layers.

**Session** lives on disk — it's the complete record of your entire conversation history, stored as JSONL files under `~/.claude/projects/`. In principle it's unbounded (limited only by disk space), survives after you close the terminal, and can be restored, rolled back, or branched.

**Context Window** lives in memory — it's the slice of the conversation packed into each API request, subject to the Claude model's hard limit (around 200K tokens). It's reassembled for each API call and vanishes when the call ends.

The relationship: Session can be much larger than Context Window. When Session gets too long, the system has to **extract** the most relevant parts from Session history to stuff into the Context Window. That's why the six-layer compression mechanism exists — it's not really "compressing" Session (Session files stay intact), it's fitting as much useful information as possible into the limited Context Window.

Analogy: Session is your full diary (sitting on the bookshelf), Context Window is the sticky-note pad you carry to today's meeting (only what's most relevant). You bring the sticky notes to the meeting not because the diary was lost, but because the diary is too thick to carry.

qa.q: CLAUDE.md 是什么？它和系统提示词（System Prompt）有什么关系？
qa.qEn: What is CLAUDE.md? How does it relate to the System Prompt?
qa.a:
CLAUDE.md 是你（或团队）用 Markdown 写的项目说明文件，告诉 Claude 关于这个项目的规则、约定和背景——比如"这个项目用 Python 3.11，测试框架用 pytest，不要修改 config/ 目录下的文件"。

它和 System Prompt 的关系是：CLAUDE.md 的内容会被加载进 System Prompt。换句话说，CLAUDE.md 是 System Prompt 的一个来源，最终会被放进每次 API 调用的背景信息包里。

CLAUDE.md 有六种来源（项目级、本地级、用户级、企业管理级、父目录级、工作区根目录级），系统会把所有找到的 CLAUDE.md 内容合并。这意味着你可以在项目根目录放一个对所有人可见的说明，同时在 .claude/CLAUDE.local.md 里放只有你自己看的个人设置。

实用建议：CLAUDE.md 是你影响 Claude 行为最直接的工具，不需要改代码或配置文件。把项目的关键约束、常用命令、禁止操作写进去，Claude 每次启动都会"读手册"。这比在每次对话开头重复说明要高效得多。
qa.aEn:
CLAUDE.md is a Markdown file you (or your team) write to describe the project to Claude — the project's rules, conventions, and background. For example: "This project uses Python 3.11, tests run under pytest, don't modify files in `config/`."

Its relationship to the System Prompt: **CLAUDE.md content gets loaded into the System Prompt**. In other words, CLAUDE.md is one of the sources that feeds the System Prompt and ends up in the background-information packet on every API call.

CLAUDE.md comes from six possible locations (project-level, local-level, user-level, enterprise-managed-level, parent-directory-level, workspace-root-level), and the system merges every CLAUDE.md it finds. That means you can drop a team-visible README-style file at the project root while keeping a personal-only `.claude/CLAUDE.local.md` for your own settings.

Practical tip: CLAUDE.md is your most direct lever for shaping Claude's behavior — no code or config changes needed. Put the project's key constraints, common commands, and forbidden operations in it; Claude "reads the manual" every time it starts. That's far more efficient than repeating the instructions at the start of every conversation.

qa.q: Hook（钩子）和 Tool（工具）有什么本质区别？什么时候应该用 Hook，什么时候用 Tool？
qa.qEn: What's the fundamental difference between a Hook and a Tool? When should I use which?
qa.a:
本质区别在于触发方式和目的：Tool 是 AI 主动发起的操作请求（"我要读这个文件"），由 AI 决定何时调用；Hook 是系统自动在关键节点触发的回调（"工具执行完毕了，通知一下"），由系统决定何时触发，用户预先设置。

还有一个关键区别：Hook 可以阻止操作（退出码 2 = 拦截），Tool 不能阻止其他操作。这使 Hook 天然适合做"守门员"——在工具执行前检查它、拦截危险操作、记录审计日志。

什么时候用哪个？如果你想"让 Claude 能做某件新事"——用 Tool（或者通过 MCP 接入外部工具）；如果你想"在 Claude 做某件事时自动执行某个动作"——用 Hook。

具体例子：你想让 Claude 能发 Slack 通知（新能力）→ 接入一个 MCP 工具；你想在 Claude 每次修改完文件后自动运行代码格式化工具 → 设置一个 PostToolUse Hook。两者都是扩展系统能力，但扩展的"层面"不同。
qa.aEn:
The fundamental difference is in trigger and purpose. A **Tool** is an operation request the AI itself initiates ("I want to read this file") — the AI decides when to call it. A **Hook** is a callback the system automatically fires at key events ("a tool just finished, let me notify you") — the system decides when to fire, and the user pre-configures it.

Another crucial difference: **Hooks can block operations** (exit code 2 = intercept), Tools can't block other tools. This makes Hooks naturally suited as "gatekeepers" — inspecting a tool before it runs, intercepting dangerous operations, writing audit logs.

When to use which? If you want "to let Claude do something new" — use a Tool (or wire in an external tool via MCP). If you want "something to happen automatically when Claude does something" — use a Hook.

Concrete examples: you want Claude to be able to send Slack notifications (new capability) → wire in an MCP tool. You want a code formatter to run automatically after every file edit → set up a PostToolUse Hook. Both extend system capabilities, but at different layers.

qa.q: Prompt Cache（提示词缓存）节省的 10 倍费用是怎么来的？它真的能在实际使用中生效吗？
qa.qEn: Where does the 10x savings from Prompt Cache come from? Does it actually kick in in real use?
qa.a:
10 倍差距来自 Anthropic 的 API 定价：普通输入 Token 约 $15/百万，缓存命中的 Token 只要 $1.5/百万（以 Claude Opus 4.6 为例）。这不是估算，是官方定价。

Prompt Cache 的工作原理：如果两次 API 请求的前缀完全一致（逐 Token 比较），重复部分不需要重新处理，只需用缓存结果，计费用缓存价格。

在实际使用中能否生效，取决于"前缀稳定性"。Claude Code 的 System Prompt 结构是专门为缓存命中率优化设计的——最稳定的内容（工具描述、角色定义）放最前面，最容易变化的内容（当前 git 状态、最新消息）放最后面。这样，前面那一大段稳定内容就可以持续命中缓存。

对一个真实工作会话来说，System Prompt 有 15,000-34,000 Token，如果这部分每次都能命中缓存（在连续的多轮对话中通常如此），一天下来节省的费用是相当可观的。但如果你每次打开新会话、或者频繁改动 CLAUDE.md，缓存会被重置，节省效果大打折扣。
qa.aEn:
The 10x gap comes from Anthropic's API pricing: a regular input token costs about $15 per million, while a cache-hit token costs only $1.5 per million (using Claude Opus 4.6 as an example). This isn't estimated — it's the published rate.

How Prompt Cache works: if two API requests share a completely identical prefix (compared token by token), the duplicate portion doesn't need to be reprocessed — the cached result is reused and billed at the cache price.

Whether it kicks in in practice depends on **prefix stability**. Claude Code's System Prompt is structured specifically to maximize cache hit rate — the most stable content (tool descriptions, role definitions) goes at the very front, the most variable content (current git state, latest messages) goes at the back. That way, the long stable preamble keeps hitting the cache.

For a real working session, the System Prompt is 15,000-34,000 tokens. If that block hits the cache on every call (which it usually does across continuous multi-turn conversations), the daily savings are substantial. But if you constantly open new sessions or modify CLAUDE.md frequently, the cache resets and the savings shrink dramatically.

qa.q: Prompt Cache 和 Context Compression（上下文压缩）之间的"互相矛盾"是什么意思？
qa.qEn: What does it mean that Prompt Cache and Context Compression are "in tension" with each other?
qa.a:
这是系统设计里一个真实的张力，值得认真理解。

Prompt Cache 的工作前提是：前缀必须保持完全不变，才能命中缓存。一旦你改动了消息历史的任何部分，缓存就失效了。

上下文压缩做的恰恰是改动消息历史——把旧消息改写成摘要、删除冗余内容、折叠历史。这会直接破坏之前建立的缓存前缀，导致后续请求全部缓存未命中，成本飙升。

所以每次触发上下文压缩，实际上有两个代价：一是压缩操作本身的计算成本，二是压缩破坏了缓存，接下来一段时间要全价支付。这就是为什么系统不会频繁压缩——压缩是"不得不用的最后手段"，而不是"用起来没成本的随意操作"。

系统在两者之间寻找平衡的方式是：尽可能晚地触发压缩（维持缓存命中率），但在触发压缩时尽量彻底（一次压缩到位，减少下次再压缩的频率）。这个权衡没有完美答案，是对话长度和成本效率之间的经典工程取舍。
qa.aEn:
This is a real design tension, and it's worth understanding seriously.

Prompt Cache only works if the prefix stays **exactly** unchanged to register a hit. The moment you modify any part of the message history, the cache is invalidated.

Context compression does exactly that — modifies the message history: rewriting old messages into summaries, deleting redundant content, folding history. This directly destroys the cache prefix built up before, causing subsequent requests to all miss the cache and sending cost skyward.

So every compression event actually carries two costs: (1) the computational cost of the compression operation itself, and (2) the cache is destroyed, so you pay full price for a while afterward. That's why the system doesn't compress frequently — compression is a **last-resort lever**, not a cost-free operation to invoke casually.

The system balances between the two by: triggering compression as late as possible (to preserve cache hit rate), but being as thorough as possible when it does trigger (compressing deeply in one pass to reduce how soon the next compression will be needed). There's no perfect answer — it's a classic engineering trade-off between conversation length and cost efficiency.

qa.q: 章节里提到"tengu"是 Claude Code 的内部项目代号——为什么工程团队要给产品起代号？这有什么意义？
qa.qEn: The chapter mentions "tengu" as Claude Code's internal code name — why do engineering teams give products code names?
qa.a:
内部代号（Code Name）在大型软件项目里非常普遍，有几个实际原因：

第一，保密性。在产品正式发布前，内部代号避免在公开场合（会议、邮件、代码提交）直接使用产品名，降低信息泄露风险。

第二，功能标记的稳定性。产品名字可能改变（营销部门随时可能重命名），但代码里用了产品名的函数名、分析事件、Feature flag 就要跟着改。用内部代号，代码可以保持稳定，即使外部名字变了。

第三，内部文化认同。代号有时成为团队凝聚力的符号，"我们在做 tengu"比"我们在做 Claude Code"更有一种特殊归属感。

在 Claude Code 的代码里，`tengu` 出现在函数命名（如 `tengu.recordEvent`）、分析事件名（如 `tengu_query_started`）和 Feature flag 键名里。了解这个代号可以帮你读懂源码里那些"为什么有个词叫 tengu 到处出现"的困惑。它不是一个你需要理解的功能概念，而是一个历史背景知识，知道了可以减少阅读源码时的困惑。
qa.aEn:
Internal code names are very common on large software projects, for several practical reasons.

**Confidentiality.** Before a product officially launches, a code name keeps the real product name out of public contexts (meetings, emails, commits), reducing leak risk.

**Stability of feature markers.** Product names can change (marketing can rename anything at any time), and code that uses the product name in function names, analytics events, or feature flags all has to follow. With a code name, code stays stable even if the external name changes.

**Internal culture and identity.** A code name sometimes becomes a symbol of team cohesion — "we're shipping tengu" carries a kind of belonging that "we're shipping Claude Code" doesn't.

In Claude Code's source, `tengu` shows up in function names (e.g., `tengu.recordEvent`), analytics event names (e.g., `tengu_query_started`), and feature-flag keys. Knowing this code name helps you resolve the confusion of "why is this word *tengu* all over the place?" It's not a feature concept you need to understand — it's background trivia that reduces friction while reading the source.

qa.q: 系统有九层配置（Settings 系统），这真的有必要吗？普通用户和企业用户的配置体验有什么不同？
qa.qEn: The system has nine configuration layers (Settings). Is that really necessary? What's the difference in experience between individual and enterprise users?
qa.a:
九层配置听起来很复杂，但它服务于非常不同的用户群，有其存在的合理性。

从普通个人用户的角度：你根本感受不到九层——你只会接触到用户配置（~/.claude/settings.json 或 UI 里的设置界面）。绝大多数用户一辈子只改过几个选项（比如"是否自动批准工具调用"），其他层对他们透明。

从企业管理员的角度：九层就非常重要了。企业需要确保所有开发者机器上的 Claude Code 都遵守公司安全策略（比如"不允许访问某些目录"、"不允许使用某些工具"），即使开发者自己想改也改不了——这就是"企业策略层"（最高优先级，无法被其他层覆盖）的价值。它等于给了 IT 管理员一个远程控制旋钮。

九层的设计哲学是：高优先级层（企业策略）能覆盖低优先级层（用户偏好），但不是所有配置都有覆盖权。这让"自上而下的管控"和"自下而上的个性化"在同一个系统里共存。普通用户不会感受到它的存在，但企业场景里这是必须有的能力。如果只有一层配置，要么企业管不了员工，要么员工没法个性化——两个极端都不行。
qa.aEn:
Nine layers of configuration sounds elaborate, but it serves very different user segments, and the design is justified.

From an individual user's perspective: you never feel the nine layers at all — you only ever touch user config (`~/.claude/settings.json` or the UI's settings panel). The vast majority of users change only a handful of options in their lifetime (e.g., "auto-approve tool calls"), and the other layers are invisible.

From an enterprise admin's perspective: nine layers become essential. The enterprise needs every developer's Claude Code to follow company security policy (e.g., "no access to certain directories," "no use of certain tools"), and developers shouldn't be able to override it even if they try — that's the value of the **enterprise-policy layer** (highest priority, unoverridable by any other layer). It's essentially a remote-control knob for the IT admin.

The design philosophy: higher-priority layers (enterprise policy) can override lower-priority layers (user preferences), but not every setting has override authority. This lets **top-down control** and **bottom-up personalization** coexist in the same system. Individual users don't notice it; enterprise scenarios can't live without it. If there were only one configuration layer, either enterprises couldn't govern their developers or developers couldn't personalize — both extremes fail.

qa.q: 这章的"常见混淆辨析"里有一条说 Plugin 可以包含 MCP——那 Plugin 和 MCP 到底是什么关系？为什么搞这么复杂？
qa.qEn: In the "Common Confusions" section, it says a Plugin can contain MCP. So what's the real relationship between Plugin and MCP? Why make it so complicated?
qa.a:
理解这个关系，先要知道它们各自解决什么问题。

MCP（Model Context Protocol，模型上下文协议）是一个标准协议，定义了外部服务如何向 AI 系统提供工具。任何按 MCP 协议实现的外部服务器，都可以接入支持 MCP 的 AI 系统（不只是 Claude Code）。它是跨系统的通用接口标准，类比 USB 协议——只要按标准做，就能接进去。

Plugin（插件）是 Claude Code 自己的包管理概念，比 MCP 粒度更粗。一个 Plugin 可以包含多种组件：工具、Skills（可复用的 AI 行为脚本）、Hooks（事件钩子）、MCP 配置、以及斜杠命令。Plugin 是"功能包"，MCP 是其中可能包含的一类组件。

为什么搞这么复杂？因为它们解决的是不同层次的问题。MCP 解决的是"怎么连接外部工具"，Plugin 解决的是"怎么打包和分发一套完整的扩展功能"。一个完整的扩展（比如"接入 Jira 的工作流"）可能需要 MCP 工具（查询 Jira）+ Hooks（每次提交时自动更新 Jira 状态）+ Commands（/jira create 命令）——这些放在一个 Plugin 包里，比分散配置要方便很多。复杂度换来的是表达能力。
qa.aEn:
To understand the relationship, first understand what each one solves.

**MCP (Model Context Protocol)** is a standard protocol defining how external services expose tools to AI systems. Any external server implementing MCP can plug into any MCP-aware AI system — not just Claude Code. It's a cross-system universal interface standard, analogous to USB — as long as you follow the spec, you can plug in.

**Plugin** is Claude Code's own package-management concept, at a coarser granularity than MCP. A Plugin can bundle multiple components: tools, Skills (reusable AI behavior scripts), Hooks (event hooks), MCP configuration, and slash commands. A Plugin is a "feature bundle"; MCP is one of the component types a Plugin might contain.

Why make it this complicated? Because they solve problems at different levels. MCP solves "how to connect to external tools." Plugin solves "how to package and distribute a complete set of extension features." A complete extension (e.g., "integrate the Jira workflow") might need MCP tools (to query Jira) + Hooks (auto-update Jira status on each commit) + Commands (`/jira create`) — bundling those into one Plugin is far more convenient than configuring them piecemeal. The complexity buys expressive power.
